zh.Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales: 威爾士布洛可爾布洛可 卡素
zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke於大約1194年出生在威爾士布洛可爾布洛可 卡素時，他的父親Robert le Waleys37歲，母親Dionisia le Poitevin25歲。他和Matilda至少有1個女兒。他去世於大約1219年在他的家鄉享年27歲。

zh-hans.Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales: 威尔士布洛可爾布洛可 卡素
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke于大约1194年出生在威尔士布洛可爾布洛可 卡素时，他的父亲Robert le Waleys37岁，母亲Dionisia le Poitevin25岁。他和Matilda至少有1个女儿。他去世于大约1219年在他的家乡享年27岁。

nameMeanings.name: Waleys,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English:

habitational name from Wales near Sheffield (South Yorkshire). The placename means ‘The Welshmen’ and denoted a settlement of Brittonic speakers living on the boundary between the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms of Northumbria and Mercia (from Old English wēalas, walas, plural of walh, wealh, originally meaning ‘Roman citizen’ and referring to the native British population, but in Old English this term later came to mean ‘serf, unfree person, foreigner’ or ‘Welshman’). See also Wallis .
habitational name from Wales, the country named from Old English wēalas ‘foreigners’ (especially those speaking Celtic or Romance languages). This meaning of the surname is quite rare.

variants.name: Wailes
meanings: English: variant of   Wales  .

variants.name: Wallis
meanings: Scottish and English: from Anglo-Norman French Waleis, Walais ‘Welshman’, also sometimes ‘Breton’ (from Old English wēalas, walas, plural of walh, wealh, originally meaning a ‘Roman citizen’ and referring to the native British population, but in Old English this term later came to mean ‘serf, unfree person, foreigner’ or ‘Welshman’). In western and central England the medieval reference is clearly to Welshmen. In Norfolk and Lincolnshire, the reference was probably to Bretons, many of whom settled in the eastern counties after the Conquest. Compare Welsh , Walsh . The idea that the Scottish surname refers to the Welsh-speaking Britons of Strathclyde is erroneous.

nameMeanings.name: Baron
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: French, English, Dutch, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and Croatian; Breton (also Le Baron): from Old French barun, baron, Middle English barun, baroun, Middle Dutch baroen, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and dialectal Croatian baron ‘baron’ (i.e. a nobleman ranking below an earl and above a knight), a title of nobility which was in England also awarded to certain freemen of the cities of London and York and of the Cinque Ports. As a surname it is unlikely to be a status name denoting a person of rank. Generally, it referred to service in a baronial household or was acquired as a nickname by a peasant who had ideas above his station or applied to a man showing characteristics of a nobleman. See also 3 and 4 below, compare Barron and Lebaron .
French: habitational name from (Le) Baron, the name of several places in various parts of France. Compare   Lebaron  .
German: status name for a freeman or baron, from barūn ‘imperial or church official’, a loanword in Middle High German from Old French (see 1 above).

variants.name: Barron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Jesus, Manuel, Francisco, Carlos, Miguel, Raul, Pedro, Ruben, Alfredo, Luis.

Scottish: status name from Scots baron, which in the Highlands denoted a small proprietary landholder. In Angus it alluded specifically to a tenant of one of the small baronies attached to the Abbey of Coupar Angus. The surname was taken by Scottish migrants to Ulster. Compare Baron .
Scottish: shortened form of McBarron, from Scottish Gaelic Mac a' Bharain ‘son of the baron’. See Baron and 1 above.

variants.name: Boron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Krystyna, Edyta, Leszek, Mieczyslaw, Mieczyslawa, Wladyslaw.
Polish (Boroń): from a pet form of the personal names Borzysław or Borzymir, formed with the element bor ‘to fight’ (from Old Slavic boriti se).
French: habitational name from a place so called in Franche-Comté.

variants.name: Baro
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Mario, Manuel, Ana, Angel, Carlos, Cesar, Fernando, Jesus, Jose, Orlando, Ramon, Rosalia.  Italian Guido, Angelo, Carmelo, Federico, Lucio.
Catalan (Baró): status name from baró ‘free man, baron’ (see Baron 1), or a habitational name from a place so named.
Hungarian (Báró): status name from báró ‘baron’. In the form Baró, it could be from a pet form of the personal names Barabás (see Barabas ) or Bertalan.

variants.name: Barona
meanings: Spanish: from the feminine form of Barón ‘baron’ (see Baron ), applied as a nickname or as an occupational name for a member of the household of a baroness.
Italian: probably from the feminine form of   Barone  . The name is concentrated in Sondrio province (Lombardy).

variants.name: Barren
meanings: English: probably a variant of   Baron   or   Barran  .

nameMeanings.name: Waleys
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English:

habitational name from Wales near Sheffield (South Yorkshire). The placename means ‘The Welshmen’ and denoted a settlement of Brittonic speakers living on the boundary between the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms of Northumbria and Mercia (from Old English wēalas, walas, plural of walh, wealh, originally meaning ‘Roman citizen’ and referring to the native British population, but in Old English this term later came to mean ‘serf, unfree person, foreigner’ or ‘Welshman’). See also Wallis .
habitational name from Wales, the country named from Old English wēalas ‘foreigners’ (especially those speaking Celtic or Romance languages). This meaning of the surname is quite rare.

variants.name: Wailes
meanings: English: variant of   Wales  .

variants.name: Wallis
meanings: Scottish and English: from Anglo-Norman French Waleis, Walais ‘Welshman’, also sometimes ‘Breton’ (from Old English wēalas, walas, plural of walh, wealh, originally meaning a ‘Roman citizen’ and referring to the native British population, but in Old English this term later came to mean ‘serf, unfree person, foreigner’ or ‘Welshman’). In western and central England the medieval reference is clearly to Welshmen. In Norfolk and Lincolnshire, the reference was probably to Bretons, many of whom settled in the eastern counties after the Conquest. Compare Welsh , Walsh . The idea that the Scottish surname refers to the Welsh-speaking Britons of Strathclyde is erroneous.

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Kyong, Kyong Suk, Young, Chae, Chi Young, Hang, Kil, Rin, Sang Hun, Chang, Chong, Insoo, Kum, Sun Young, Yom, Young Hwan.  Cambodian Kiwan, Oeurn, Phoeun, Pich, Saroeuth, Seung, Sophath, Sophea, Yoeun, Phan, Son.  Indian Shanti, Harendra.
Indian (Uttar Pradesh): Brahmin name, from Sanskrit om, the Hindu sacred and mystic syllable uttered at the beginning of prayers.
Cambodian: written ឱម, corresponding to the exclamation of solemn affirmation, of Sanskrit origin (see 1 above), chanted by Buddhist monks.

variants.name: Oum
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Heang, Hye, Kam, Kan, Kheng, Khin, Kun, Sang, Sok, Phong, Yom, Yoo, Youn, Cuong, Phal, Samnang, Tin.  Cambodian Sokha, Chheang, Choeun, Moeun, Roeun, Sambath, Samoeun, Savuth, Soeun, Sopheap.
Cambodian: written អ៊ុំ, from a Khmer word denoting an elder sibling of one's parent (i.e. ‘uncle’ or ‘aunt’). Compare   Om   3 and   Um   2.

nameMeanings.name: Pembroke
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Irish: habitational name from Pembroke (Welsh Penfro, from Welsh pen ‘end’ + brog ‘land’) in southwest Wales. Pembroke Castle was of strategic importance in medieval and Tudor times because of its commanding position at the western end of the Bristol Channel and its control of seaborne traffic between Britain and the south of Ireland. It was from Pembroke that Richard de Clare, Earl of Pembroke, nicknamed ‘Strongbow’, launched an expedition to Wexford in 1169–70, which brought many English and Welsh surnames to Ireland.

variants.name: Pembrook
meanings: Irish and English: variant of   Pembroke  .

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of Simon , now rare, but more common in the Middle Ages, when it gave rise to the surnames Simms and Simpson.

variants.name: Simon
meanings: Usual English form of Simeon , borne in the New Testament by various characters: two apostles, a brother of Jesus, a Pharisee, a leper, a tanner, a sorcerer (who offered money for the gifts of the Holy Ghost, giving rise to the term simony), and the man who carried Jesus's cross to the Crucifixion.

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

nameMeanings.name: II
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of   Violet  ,   Vivien  , or   Vivian .

variants.name: Vivien
meanings: Earlier generally taken as a variant of the boy's name Vivian , but now also rarely used for boys. This spelling was quite common in Old French. Its use as a girl's name in the English-speaking world was influenced by Tennyson's Merlin and Vivien ( 1859 ). This name, from Arthurian legend, may represent an altered form of a Celtic name (perhaps akin to the Irish Gaelic name Béibhinn ‘white lady’, pronounced ‘bee-veen’). The actress Vivien Leigh ( 1913–67 ) was christened Vivian.

variants.name: Violet
meanings: From the name of the flower (Old French violette, Late Latin violetta, a diminutive of viola). This was one of the earliest flower names to become popular in Britain, being used as early as 1700 and becoming well established in the 19th century, although it is now somewhat out of favour.

variants.name: Vivian
meanings: Originally a boy's name, from an Old French form of the Latin name Vivianus (probably a derivative of vivus ‘alive’), but now more frequent as a girl's name. The name was borne by a 5th-century bishop of Saintes in western France, remembered for protecting his people during the invasion of the Visigoths.

nameMeanings.name: Le
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: ‘Bright’.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke was born about 1194, in Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales, his father, Robert le Waleys, was 37 and his mother, Dionisia le Poitevin, was 25. He had at least 1 daughter with Matilda. He died about 1219, in his hometown, at the age of 27.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke est né environ 1194, à Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Galles, son père, Robert le Waleys, avait 37 ans et sa mère, Dionisia le Poitevin, avait 25 ans. Il a eu au moins 1 fille avec Matilda. Il est décédé environ 1219, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 27 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke um 1194 in Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales geboren wurde, war sein Vater Robert le Waleys 37 und seine Mutter Dionisia le Poitevin 25. Er hatte mit Matilda mindestens 1 Tochter. Er starb um 1219 im Alter von 27 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke è nato il circa 1194 nel Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Galles, il suo padre, Robert le Waleys, aveva 37 anni e il suo madre, Dionisia le Poitevin, aveva 25 anni. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlia con Matilda. Morì circa 1219, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 27 anni.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembrokeが約1194年にウェールズペンブロケシュアイアペンブロク カストルで生まれた時，父Robert le Waleysは37歳，母Dionisia le Poitevinは25歳であった。彼はMatildaとの間に少なくとも1人の娘をもうけた。彼は約1219年に故郷で27歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke(이)가 대략 1194년 웨일스 p브로케실르 p브로크 카스틀에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Robert le Waleys(은)는 37세였고, 그의 어머니 Dionisia le Poitevin(은)는 25세였다. 그는 Matilda(와)과 적어도 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 대략 1219년 그의 고향에서 27세의 나이로 사망했다.

mg.about 1194–about 1219: tany ho any amin'ny 1194–tany ho any amin'ny 1219

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke nasceu aproximadamente 1194, em Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Gales, seu pai, Robert le Waleys, tinha 37 anos e sua mãe, Dionisia le Poitevin, tinha 25 anos. Ele teve pelo menos 1 filha com Matilda. Ele faleceu aproximadamente 1219, em sua cidade natal, com 27 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke родился около 1194 г. в местности пемброк кастл, пемброкешир, Уэльс, его отцу по имени Robert le Waleys было 37 лет, а матери, которую звали Dionisia le Poitevin, 25. У него была по крайней мере 1 дочь от Matilda. Он умер около 1219 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 27 лет.

sm.about 1194–about 1219: e tusa o le 1194–e tusa o le 1219

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Sir Thomas II Le Waleys, Baron Waleys of Pembroke nació aproximadamente 1194, en Pembroke Castle, Pembrokeshire, Gales, su papá, Robert le Waleys, tenía 37 años y su mamá, Dionisia le Poitevin, tenía 25 años. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hija con Matilda. Murió aproximadamente 1219, en su pueblo, a la edad de 27 años.
